groups.name: Theta II Petrol Family

Our rebuilt units include upgraded bearings, revised piston rings, and enhanced oil pump components to address these known issues.

groups.name: CRDi R‑Line Diesel Family
groups.failureNote: Get Quote → (per row, for developer implementation) Common failure points (expandable note): CRDi R‑Line engines are known for EGR valve clogging, DPF regeneration failures, and injector wear. Our rebuilt units include refurbished injectors, cleaned EGR systems, and updated DPF components to ensure reliable operation.

groups.name: Gamma Petrol Family
groups.failureNote: Get Quote → (per row, for developer implementation) Common failure points (expandable note): Gamma engines are known for timing chain tensioner wear, carbon buildup on GDI intake valves, and oil consumption on high‑mileage units. Our rebuilt units include upgraded tensioners, cleaned intake systems, and revised piston rings.

groups.name: Kappa Petrol Family
groups.failureNote: Get Quote → (per row, for developer implementation) Common failure points (expandable note): Kappa engines are known for timing chain stretch on early models, carbon buildup on GDI variants, and oil consumption. Our rebuilt units include upgraded chain tensioners, cleaned intake valves, and revised PCV systems.

groups.name: Nu Petrol Family
groups.failureNote: Get Quote → (per row, for developer implementation) Common failure points (expandable note): Nu engines are known for piston ring wear leading to oil consumption, carbon buildup on GDI variants, and timing chain tensioner issues. Our rebuilt units include upgraded piston rings, cleaned intake systems, and revised tensioners.

groups.name: U‑Line CRDi Diesel Family
groups.failureNote: Get Quote → (per row, for developer implementation) Common failure points (expandable note): U‑Line CRDi engines are known for EGR valve clogging, turbocharger wear on higher‑mileage units, and injector failure. Our rebuilt units include refurbished turbos, cleaned or replaced EGR systems, and tested injectors.

groups.name: Smartstream Petrol Family
groups.failureNote: Get Quote → (per row, for developer implementation) Common failure points (expandable note): Smartstream engines are known for carbon buildup on GDI variants, high‑pressure fuel pump wear, and turbocharger issues on turbo models. Our rebuilt units include cleaned intake valves, tested fuel systems, and refurbished or replaced turbo components

commonProblems.h2: Common Hyundai Engine Problems — What It Costs to Fix & When Replacement Makes Sense
commonProblems.h3: Hyundai's reputation for affordability means many owners keep their cars well beyond warranty, but certain engine families dominate replacement demand: the Theta II GDi petrol units (2.0/2.4L) and CRDi diesel engines (1.6/2.0L). Every rebuilt engine supplied through EngineMarket includes upgraded internal components designed to address the original failure point — plus a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty. The problem groups below break down real UK repair costs versus replacement, so you can decide whether a fix or a fresh engine gives you the best value for your i30, Tucson, or Santa Fe.

problems.h4: Theta II GDi Bearing Failure – Why 2.0/2.4L Petrol Engines Seize Prematurely
problems.affectedModels: Hyundai Tucson, Santa Fe, i40, Sonata (2011–2019, G4NA/G4NC/G4KJ 2.0L & 2.4L GDi)
problems.rootCause: Inadequate oil flow to the connecting rod bearings combined with tight manufacturing tolerances causes accelerated wear, especially under short-trip driving or delayed oil changes.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Fit upgraded oil catch can, high-flow oil pump gear, and switch to low-SAPS synthetic oil; does not reverse existing bearing wear. | Only viable if caught before knocking starts; extends life by ~20–30k miles if driven gently. |

| Remove engine, replace connecting rod bearings, inspect/machine crankshaft, renew oil pump and seals; requires precision torque specs. | Restores reliability if crankshaft is undamaged; not recommended if knocking has occurred for >100 miles. |

repairOptions.tier: | Full Replacement – Rebuilt Theta II Engine |
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Supply & fit of reconditioned engine with upgraded bearings, piston rings, and oil pump; bench-tested, ECU reset, 12‑month warranty included. | Best value for cars worth £4k+; eliminates recurrence risk with upgraded internals and full warranty coverage. |

problems.recommendation: On a Tucson or Santa Fe worth £4,000–£8,000, we rarely recommend bearing-only repairs once knocking is audible — the risk of hidden crank damage is too high. A rebuilt Theta II with upgraded components from our network delivers peace of mind and often costs less than a dealer-led partial repair.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt Theta II engine prices for your Tucson or Santa Fe

problems.h4: Gamma GDi Timing Chain Stretch – Why 1.0–1.6L Petrol Engines Rattle and Fail
problems.affectedModels: Hyundai i10, i20, i30, Tucson (2011–2018, G3LA/G3LC/G4FG 1.0–1.6L GDi)
problems.rootCause: Weak hydraulic tensioners and plastic guide wear cause the chain to stretch; oil starvation from infrequent changes accelerates the process, leading to timing drift.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replace timing chain, tensioner, guides, and seals; reset timing marks; recommended before rattling starts. | Adds 80,000+ miles of reliable running if done pre-failure; not viable once chain has jumped. |

| Remove head, replace bent valves, reface seats, renew head gasket; only possible if pistons/cylinders are undamaged. | Suitable for early-interference events; costly and still leaves original chain weakness unaddressed. |

| Supply & fit of reconditioned engine with upgraded metal-chain kit, reinforced guides, and bench testing; 12‑month warranty included. | Most cost-effective for i30/Tucson models over 80k miles; eliminates timing-chain anxiety with upgraded internals. |

problems.recommendation: If your Gamma engine is rattling cold but still runs smoothly, a preventative chain kit from a specialist offers excellent value. Once the chain has jumped and caused valve contact, however, a rebuilt engine is almost always cheaper and more reliable than a head-only repair.
problems.cta: → Get quotes for rebuilt Gamma engine replacements for your i30 or Tucson

problems.h4: CRDi Diesel EGR/DPF Clogging – Why Oil Dilution Kills 1.6/2.0L Diesels
problems.affectedModels: Hyundai i30, ix35, Tucson, Santa Fe (2010–2020, D4FB/D4HA 1.6/2.0L CRDi)
problems.rootCause: Short-trip driving prevents DPF regeneration; soot builds in the EGR cooler and mixes with oil, causing dilution that thins lubrication and accelerates bearing wear.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Blank EGR valve, chemically clean or professionally remove DPF (where legal), flush oil system; resets fault codes. | Only stops further damage; does not repair bearings already worn by diluted oil. MOT compliance varies. |

repairOptions.tier: | Intermediate – Engine-Out EGR/DPF Service + Bearing Check |
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Remove engine, strip EGR/DPF hardware, inspect bearings and crank, replace oil pump if worn; extensive labour. | Viable only if bearing clearance is still within spec; high labour cost with no warranty on original internals. |

| Supply & fit of reconditioned CRDi with upgraded oil pump, revised EGR delete kit (where compliant), bench-tested; 12‑month warranty. | Best long-term solution for high-mileage diesels; eliminates oil-dilution risk with upgraded components and full warranty. |

problems.recommendation: For CRDi engines showing early warning signs (increased oil level, DPF light), an EGR blank and DPF clean can buy time. But if the dipstick shows fuel-thinned oil or you hear bearing knock, a rebuilt CRDi from our network is the only route that guarantees reliability and warranty protection.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt CRDi engine prices for your i30 or Santa Fe

problems.h4: Kappa T-GDi Turbo Failure – Why Small Petrol Turbos Burn Out Early
problems.affectedModels: Hyundai i10, i20, Bayon (2017–2023, G3LA/G3LB 1.0L T-GDi)
problems.rootCause: High exhaust temperatures in the compact turbo housing cause oil coking in feed lines; carbon buildup on the wastegate leads to sticking and over-boost events.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replace faulty wastegate actuator, clean carbon from linkage, reset ECU adaptation; does not address internal turbo wear. | Only suitable for boost-control faults with no shaft play or oil smoke; temporary fix if turbo internals are worn. |

| Remove old turbo, fit new/remanufactured OEM-spec unit, renew feed/return lines and gaskets, ECU adaptation. | Restores performance if no debris entered the engine; does not prevent recurrence without oil-system upgrades. |

repairOptions.tier: | Full Replacement – Rebuilt Kappa Engine with Upgraded Turbo |
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Supply & fit of reconditioned Kappa engine with upgraded turbocharger, reinforced oil feed, bench-tested; 12‑month warranty. | Most reliable option for high-mileage i10/i20; eliminates turbo-failure recurrence with upgraded components and full warranty. |

problems.recommendation: If your Kappa engine is just throwing boost codes with no smoke or noise, a specialist actuator repair can be cost-effective. But if you see blue smoke or hear turbo whine, debris has likely entered the cylinders — at that point, a rebuilt engine with an upgraded turbo is the only route that protects your investment.
problems.cta: → Get quotes for rebuilt Kappa engine replacements for your i10 or i20

problems.h4: Nu MPi Oil Consumption – Why 1.8/2.0L Petrol Engines Burn Oil Excessively
problems.affectedModels: Hyundai i30, i40, Sonata (2012–2019, G4ND/G4NJ 1.8/2.0L MPi)
problems.rootCause: Piston ring land design and valve stem seal hardening allow oil to pass into the combustion chamber; exacerbated by infrequent oil changes and short trips.

repairOptions.tier: | Minor – PCV Valve & High-Mileage Oil Regime |
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replace PCV valve, switch to high-viscosity synthetic oil, add oil catch can; monitors consumption. | May reduce consumption by 30–50% if rings are only lightly worn; does not fix mechanical wear. |

repairOptions.tier: | Intermediate – Piston Ring & Valve Seal Replacement |
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Remove engine, replace piston rings and valve stem seals, hone cylinders if needed; extensive labour with precision assembly. | Only viable if cylinder bores are undamaged; costly with no warranty on original block. |

| Supply & fit of reconditioned Nu engine with upgraded rings, revised valve seals, bench-tested; 12‑month warranty included. | Best value for i40/Sonata models over 90k miles; eliminates oil-consumption risk with upgraded internals and full warranty. |

problems.recommendation: For Nu engines consuming less than 1L per 1,000 miles, a PCV upgrade and high-mileage oil can extend life affordably. Once consumption exceeds that threshold or you see blue exhaust smoke, a rebuilt Nu engine is almost always cheaper and more reliable than an in-situ ring job.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt Nu engine prices for your i40 or Sonata

finalCta.h4: Don't Let Engine Failure Write Off Your Hyundai
finalCta.paragraph: Whether you're facing Theta II bearing knock, Gamma timing-chain rattle, or CRDi oil dilution, EngineMarket makes it simple to compare prices from trusted UK Hyundai specialists in minutes. Every engine we supply includes upgraded components that address the original failure point, bench testing for peace of mind, and a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty — with nationwide delivery or supply‑and‑fit options. Skip the main dealer premium and get back on the road with a solution that fits your budget and your car's value.
finalCta.buttonText: Compare Hyundai Engine Replacement Prices Now
finalCta.disclaimer: All prices are indicative UK market ranges based on historical quote data and repair requests from our network of engine specialists. Dealer pricing is estimated based on published main dealer labour rates and genuine OEM parts pricing. Specialist pricing reflects actual quote data from EngineMarket's UK network. Actual costs vary depending on damage severity, vehicle condition, variant, and workshop location within the UK. Use our free comparison tool to get tailored quotes for your specific Hyundai model and engine code. Every engine supplied through EngineMarket carries a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty — always confirm exact terms with your chosen supplier.

engineTypes.h2: Hyundai Engine Types – Options, Costs & What to Expect
engineTypes.intro: Every Hyundai engine type available through our UK network, with typical price ranges and recommendations based on your vehicle's age, condition and your budget.

types.title: Hyundai Reconditioned Engines (Recon)
types.description: A stripped, cleaned and fully inspected engine where all worn components are replaced with OEM‑spec parts. Best for owners who want factory reliability without the factory price — ideal for i30, Tucson and Santa Fe owners addressing common CRDi or Theta II wear issues.
types.priceRange: Typical price range: £1,200 – £3,500 (depending on engine code)

types.title: Hyundai Rebuilt Engines
types.description: A complete rebuild with upgraded components addressing known failure points — such as uprated bearing shells for Theta II petrol units and refurbished turbos for CRDi diesels — for a longer service life than a standard replacement.

types.title: Used Hyundai Engines
types.description: A tested, low‑mileage take‑out unit from a donor vehicle. The most affordable option when budget is the priority, with verified history and warranty available — perfect for i10, i20 and Getz owners seeking a cheap, quick solution.

types.title: Hyundai Remanufactured Engines
types.description: A factory‑spec, as‑new engine: every surface is machined, every component is replaced, and the unit is built to meet exact OEM tolerances. The premium choice for performance models or owners wanting a zero‑mile engine with maximum peace of mind.

types.title: Hyundai Refurbished Engines
types.description: A lightly serviced, cleaned, and tested engine that works reliably but hasn't had a complete rebuild. Often a pragmatic, fast solution for older or budget‑sensitive models like Lantra, Accent or early Getz where a full recon isn't economical.

types.title: Hyundai Supply & Fit Engines
types.description: Your replacement engine delivered and professionally installed by a UK Hyundai specialist. Includes removal of the old unit, fitting, fluids, and a workmanship warranty — the simplest way to get back on the road without arranging separate garage labour.
types.priceRange: Typical added cost: £400 – £900 on top of engine price
types.cta: Find Hyundai engine supply & fit deals

engineTypes.closing: All engine types include a minimum 12‑month warranty. Prices are typical UK market ranges and depend on exact engine code, supplier, and any additional fitting services. Use our free comparison tool to get a tailored quote for your Hyundai model today.

engineSizes.h2: Hyundai Engines by Size – Find the Right Displacement for Your Model
engineSizes.intro: Every Hyundai engine size explained — with the models and engine codes that used each displacement — so you can quickly identify the correct replacement unit for your vehicle.

items.title: Hyundai 1.4L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 1.4‑litre CRDi diesel was Hyundai's entry‑level oil‑burner for small hatchbacks, most commonly fitted to the i20, i30 and ix20 from the early‑2010s. Built around the D4FC engine family, these units are generally robust but can suffer from EGR and DPF‑related issues when used predominantly on short trips — a failure pattern that often leads owners to seek replacement rather than repeated repairs
items.cta: Get quotes for Hyundai 1.4L diesel engines
commonFailurePoints: DPF regeneration failures leading to oil dilution

items.title: Hyundai 1.6L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 1.6‑litre CRDi is Hyundai's most widely fitted diesel displacement across the UK range, powering the i30, Tucson, i40 and ix35 from 2010 onward . Primarily using the D4FB and D4FE engine codes, this size offers a strong balance of economy and torque, but is prone to EGR cooler leaks and DPF‑induced oil dilution — two issues that frequently escalate into bearing wear and drive replacement demand
items.cta: Get quotes for Hyundai 1.6L diesel engines
commonFailurePoints: DPF clogging causing oil dilution and accelerated bearing wear

items.title: Hyundai 2.0L Diesel Engines
items.description: Hyundai's 2.0‑litre CRDi diesel, built around the D4HA and D4EB families, was the workhorse for larger models like the Tucson, Santa Fe and i40 throughout the 2010s . These engines deliver strong towing capability but are susceptible to turbocharger wear, high‑pressure injector failure, and EGR‑system carbon buildup — failure modes that often make a rebuilt replacement more cost‑effective than extensive in‑situ repairs
items.cta: Get quotes for Hyundai 2.0L diesel engines

items.title: Hyundai 2.2L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 2.2‑litre CRDi, sharing architecture with the 2.0L but with increased displacement for the Santa Fe and Grand Santa Fe, was Hyundai's largest diesel offering for the UK market . Using the D4HA and D4EB codes, these engines face similar failure patterns to the 2.0L but under higher thermal loads — making turbo failure, injector issues, and timing component wear the most common drivers for replacement
items.cta: Get quotes for Hyundai 2.2L diesel engines

items.title: Hyundai 1.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: Hyundai's 1.0‑litre petrol units, part of the Kappa family (G3LA/G3LB), power the i10, i20 and Bayon with both naturally‑aspirated and turbocharged (T‑GDi) variants . While the NA versions are generally reliable, the T‑GDi variants can suffer from turbocharger oil‑coking and carbon buildup on intake valves — issues that, if ignored, may lead to costly replacement rather than repair
items.cta: Compare Hyundai 1.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Hyundai 1.2L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.2‑litre Kappa G4LA is Hyundai's small‑capacity naturally‑aspirated petrol engine, fitted to the i10 and i20 from the late‑2000s through the 2010s . Known for simplicity and low running costs, these units rarely suffer catastrophic failures, but occasional coil‑pack or sensor issues can prompt owners to consider a low‑mileage used replacement for maximum value
items.cta: Compare Hyundai 1.2L petrol engine prices

items.title: Hyundai 1.4L Petrol Engines
items.description: Hyundai's 1.4‑litre petrol displacement spans both the Gamma (G4FA/G4FC) and Kappa (G4LD) families, appearing in the i20, i30 and ix20 from the late‑2000s . Early Gamma GDi variants can experience timing‑chain stretch and carbon buildup, while later MPi versions are more robust — but any 1.4L showing excessive oil consumption or rattling may warrant a rebuilt replacement
items.cta: Compare Hyundai 1.4L petrol engine prices

items.title: Hyundai 1.6L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.6‑litre Gamma G4FG and Nu G4FD engines power the i30, Tucson and i40 across the 2010s, offering a balance of performance and efficiency for UK drivers . While generally dependable, Gamma GDi variants can develop timing‑chain noise and carbon deposits, and any 1.6L showing knocking or high oil consumption should be assessed for bearing wear before committing to minor repairs
items.cta: Compare Hyundai 1.6L petrol engine prices

items.title: Hyundai 1.8L Petrol Engines
items.description: Hyundai's 1.8‑litre Nu G4NB engine was fitted to the i30 and i40 during the early‑to‑mid 2010s as a mid‑range petrol option . These units can develop excessive oil consumption due to piston‑ring and valve‑seal wear, a failure pattern that often makes a reconditioned replacement more economical than an in‑situ ring job on higher‑mileage cars
items.cta: Compare Hyundai 1.8L petrol engine prices
commonFailurePoints: Excessive oil consumption from piston‑ring and valve‑seal wear

items.title: Hyundai 2.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 2.0‑litre Theta II (G4NA/G4NC/G4KJ) and Nu (G4ND) families dominate Hyundai's mid‑size petrol range, powering the Tucson, Santa Fe, i40 and Sonata from the late‑2000s . Theta II units are notorious for connecting‑rod bearing failure due to oil‑flow limitations — a catastrophic issue that almost always requires a full rebuilt replacement with upgraded internals rather than a partial repair
items.cta: Compare Hyundai 2.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Hyundai 2.4L Petrol Engines
items.description: Hyundai's 2.4‑litre Theta II G4KC engine was reserved for larger models like the Santa Fe and Sonata, offering extra torque for towing and load‑carrying . Sharing the same bearing‑design vulnerability as the 2.0L Theta II, these engines are prone to the same catastrophic bearing failure under oil‑starvation conditions — making a rebuilt unit with upgraded components the only reliable long‑term solution
items.cta: Compare Hyundai 2.4L petrol engine prices
commonFailurePoints: Connecting‑rod bearing failure (same root cause as 2.0L Theta II)

items.title: Hyundai 1.6L Hybrid Engines
items.description: Hyundai's 1.6‑litre hybrid system, combining a Kappa‑family petrol engine with an electric motor and battery pack, appears in the Ioniq, Tucson Hybrid and Kona Hybrid from 2016 onward . While the petrol component shares failure patterns with conventional 1.6L units, hybrid‑specific concerns centre on battery health and inverter reliability — making specialist assessment essential before pursuing replacement. System / motor code(s): G4LE + electric motor (TMED system)
items.cta: Enquire about Hyundai 1.6L hybrid engine availability

items.title: Hyundai Electric Motors
items.description: Hyundai's fully electric models — including the Kona Electric, Ioniq Electric and Ioniq 5/6 — use permanent‑magnet synchronous motors rather than traditional displacement engines . Motor failure is exceptionally rare, but high‑voltage battery degradation or inverter faults can necessitate specialist replacement; these systems require certified high‑voltage technicians and are not suitable for general engine‑replacement marketplaces. System / motor code(s): PE series (varies by model/year)
items.cta: Enquire about Hyundai electric motor repair

engineSizes.closing: Not sure which engine size your Hyundai needs? Enter your registration number and we'll match you to the exact engine code and displacement — instantly. All engines supplied with a minimum 12‑month warranty from UK Hyundai specialists.

fuelTypes.intro: Choose your Hyundai engine by fuel type to compare realistic replacement prices, known brand‑wide issues, and UK specialist supply – from the most popular diesel workhorses to the latest electrified powertrains.
fuelTypes.closing: Not sure which fuel type your Hyundai has? Enter your registration number above to instantly identify your model, engine code, fuel system and compatible replacement options – all backed by a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty from UK Hyundai specialists.

engineCodeDirectory.h2: Hyundai Engine Codes – Most Replaced Engines (2008–2025)
engineCodeDirectory.intro: Every major Hyundai engine code from the last two decades, grouped by family, with compatible models and typical reasons why owners seek replacement. Select your engine to compare rebuilt prices and get quotes from UK Hyundai specialists.

entries.title: G4KD 2.0L Petrol
entries.description: Widely fitted to the Hyundai Tucson (LM/TL), ix35, and Sonata (YF/LF) between 2009 and 2019. Owners frequently report premature main bearing wear and sudden oil pressure drops that escalate into complete engine seizure. UK specialists rebuild these units with uprated main and rod bearings, high-flow oil pumps, and reinforced piston ring sets.
entries.cta: Compare G4KD Hyundai engine prices

entries.title: G4KE 2.4L Petrol
entries.description: Standard in the Santa Fe (CM/DM) and Sonata (LF) from 2006 through 2018. High-mileage examples commonly suffer from connecting rod bearing failure and excessive oil consumption under towing loads. Quality rebuilt versions install reinforced crankshaft bearings, revised oil pump assemblies, and upgraded PCV valves.
entries.cta: Get quotes for G4KE Hyundai engine replacement

entries.title: G4NC 2.0L Petrol
entries.description: Early Theta II variant found in the first-generation Tucson (LM) and early i40 (VF) between 2004 and 2010. Shares the same timing tensioner wear and bearing clearance vulnerabilities as later Theta units. Reconditioned replacements address these weaknesses with hardened bearing shells, new chain guides, and full gasket renewals.
entries.cta: Check G4NC Hyundai engine availability

entries.title: G4KF 2.0L Petrol
entries.description: Alternative 2.0L Theta code used in the i40 and early Tucson models from 2004 to 2012. Known for oil pump pressure loss and accelerated timing chain stretch under frequent short-trip driving. Specialist rebuilds supply revised high-pressure oil pumps, uprated tensioner kits, and fully machined cylinder bores.
entries.cta: Find rebuilt G4KF Hyundai engines

entries.title: D4HA 2.0L Diesel
entries.description: Powers the Tucson (TL/NX4), i40, and ix35 across the UK market from 2009 to present. EGR valve clogging and turbocharger actuator failures are the primary reasons these units are removed. Rebuilt D4HA engines include refurbished turbochargers, chemically cleaned EGR coolers, and new injector sealing kits.
entries.cta: Get quotes for D4HA Hyundai diesel engine

entries.title: D4HB 2.2L Diesel
entries.description: The workhorse CRDi unit fitted to the Santa Fe (CM/DM) and Grand Santa Fe from 2006 onwards. Long-distance and towing use typically leads to injector tip wear and severe DPF backpressure. Reconditioned units deliver fully bench-tested injectors, revised DPF housing assemblies, and upgraded oil filtration systems.
entries.cta: Compare D4HB Hyundai engine prices

entries.title: D4EB 2.0L Diesel
entries.description: Found in early Sonata and first-generation i40 models between 2006 and 2014. Turbo bearing failure and timing belt tensioner collapse frequently trigger full replacement requests. Quality rebuilds install heavy-duty turbo bearings, complete timing kit upgrades, and refreshed cylinder head cooling channels.
entries.cta: Enquire about D4EB Hyundai engine supply

entries.title: D4FC 1.4L Diesel
entries.description: Entry-level CRDi powering the i20 and early i30 from 2008 to 2015. Fuel injector sticking and EGR matrix faults drive the majority of replacement enquiries. Specialist rebuilds replace faulty injectors with recalibrated units, service the EGR matrix, and include new high-pressure fuel pumps.
entries.cta: Check D4FC Hyundai engine replacement quotes

entries.title: D4FB 1.6L Diesel
entries.description: Extremely common across the i30 (FD/GD), Kona, and Tucson from 2009 to 2020. Turbocharger shaft play and EGR valve sticking severely compromise drivability over time. Quality rebuilt units supply dynamically balanced turbos, new EGR control assemblies, and upgraded timing chain kits.
entries.cta: Get quotes for D4FB Hyundai engine replacement

entries.title: D4FD 1.6L Diesel
entries.description: Alternative variant widely used in the Elantra and i30 hatchback between 2007 and 2017. Exhibits similar turbo and injector wear patterns to the D4FB but often suffers from earlier high-pressure fuel pump degradation. Reconditioned examples feature remanufactured turbos, new piston rings, and refreshed fuel delivery components.
entries.cta: Compare D4FD Hyundai diesel engine costs

entries.title: D4FE 1.7L Diesel
entries.description: Powers the i40, second-generation Tucson, and early Kona from 2011 to 2019. Injector seat cracking and DPF saturation during urban driving are well-documented failure triggers. Rebuilt units address seating surfaces with hardened inserts, supply new DPF differential pressure sensors, and include full service kits.
entries.cta: Find rebuilt D4FE Hyundai engines

entries.title: D4EA 2.0L Diesel
entries.description: Legacy CRDi found in the Trajet, early i40, and first-generation Santa Fe from 2000 to 2010. Cylinder head gasket failure and turbo oil seal leaks remain the dominant reasons for replacement. Rebuilt versions install multi-layer steel gaskets, upgraded turbo seals, and reinforced cylinder head studs.
entries.cta: Enquire about D4EA Hyundai engine quotes

entries.title: G4FC 1.6L Petrol
entries.description: Core engine for the i30, Elantra, and ix35 across the 2006–2017 production window. Timing chain stretch and carbon buildup on direct-injection valves routinely necessitate full replacement. Rebuilt Gamma units include uprated hydraulic tensioners, walnut-blasted intake ports, and revised valve springs.
entries.cta: Compare G4FC Hyundai engine prices

entries.title: G4FJ 1.6L Turbo Petrol
entries.description: Performance variant fitted to the Veloster, i30 N-Line, and Kona from 2011 to 2020. Turbo oil starvation and piston ring wear under high boost are frequent failure catalysts. Specialist rebuilds supply reinforced turbo cores, upgraded scavenge oil pumps, and low-friction piston ring sets.
entries.cta: Get quotes for G4FJ Hyundai turbo engine

entries.title: G4FA 1.4L Petrol
entries.description: Common in the i20, Accent, and early i30 hatchback between 2008 and 2018. Timing chain rattle and excessive oil consumption on high-mileage examples drive replacement volume. Reconditioned versions replace hydraulic tensioners, install new oil control rings, and include full timing chain kits.
entries.cta: Check G4FA Hyundai engine availability

entries.title: G4FG 1.6L Petrol
entries.description: Updated MPI/GDI Gamma iteration used in the Tucson (TL) and i30 from 2013 to 2020. Reduced timing chain issues but still highly prone to GDI carbon deposits affecting idle quality. Rebuilds feature chemically cleaned cylinder heads, new chain guides, and updated PCV breather systems.
entries.cta: Find rebuilt G4FG Hyundai engines

entries.title: G4NA 1.8L Petrol
entries.description: Widely deployed in the i40, i30, and ix35 from 2010 through 2019. Famous for factory piston ring wear that leads to heavy oil consumption and eventual cylinder scoring. Quality rebuilt units install redesigned ring packs, precision-honed cylinder walls, and revised crankshaft bearings.
entries.cta: Get quotes for G4NA Hyundai engine replacement

entries.title: G4NJ 2.0L Petrol
entries.description: Found in the i40, Sonata, and Tucson from 2010 to 2020. Shares the Nu family ring wear vulnerability and suffers from carbon-induced pre-ignition. Reconditioned examples supply upgraded piston assemblies, enhanced cylinder cooling jackets, and complete gasket renewal packs.
entries.cta: Compare G4NJ Hyundai engine prices

entries.title: G4NH 2.0L Petrol
entries.description: Higher-compression Nu variant powering later Sonata and i40 saloons from 2014 to 2019. Knock sensor faults and combustion chamber carbon buildup frequently trigger limp mode. Rebuilt units include new knock sensors, mechanically cleaned combustion chambers, and revised oil pump gears.
entries.cta: Check G4NH Hyundai engine quotes

entries.title: G4NK 2.0L Petrol
entries.description: MPI iteration of the Nu family used in budget-spec i40 and early Tucson trims 2012–2018. Timing chain stretch and valve guide wear are the most reported catastrophic failure points. Specialist rebuilds fit heavy-duty tensioners, new bronze valve guides, and complete timing gear replacements.
entries.cta: Enquire about G4NK Hyundai engine supply

entries.title: G3LA 1.0L Petrol
entries.description: Powers the i10 and early i20 city cars from 2008 to 2016. Early production models suffer from timing chain rattle and cold-start oil consumption. Rebuilds replace faulty tensioner pads, install updated oil pump rotors, and include new valve stem seals.
entries.cta: Get quotes for G3LA Hyundai replacement engine

entries.title: G4LA 1.2L Petrol
entries.description: Extremely widespread in the i10, i20, and Bayon from 2011 to present. Early timing tensioner wear and GDI carbon deposits drive steady replacement demand. Specialist rebuilds fit heavy-duty tensioners, clean intake valves, and renew fuel injectors to factory tolerances.
entries.cta: Compare G4LA Hyundai engine prices

entries.title: G4LC 1.0L Turbo Petrol
entries.description: Found in the i20, Kona, and i30 from 2015 onwards. Turbo bearing wear and high-pressure fuel pump strain under urban driving patterns are common. Rebuilt units supply dynamically balanced turbos, tested high-pressure fuel pumps, and reinforced cylinder liners.
entries.cta: Check G4LC Hyundai turbo engine costs

entries.title: G4LD 1.0L Petrol
entries.description: Naturally aspirated Kappa variant used in later i10 and i20 models 2014–2022. Shares timing chain tensioner wear and piston ring glazing issues with turbo siblings. Quality rebuilds replace tensioner arms, install updated oil control rings, and include full gasket overhaul kits. Closing line (Block A): Can’t find your engine code listed above? Use our full Hyundai engine code directory below — every code from the last 25 years — or enter your registration number for an instant match to the correct replacement engine with warranty.
entries.cta: Find rebuilt G4LD Hyundai engines

engineCodeDirectory.closing: Can’t find your engine code listed above? Use our full Hyundai engine code directory below — every code from the last 25 years — or enter your registration number for an instant match to the correct replacement engine with warranty.

directory.h3: Full Hyundai Engine Code Directory (All Years)
directory.label: “View all Hyundai engine codes (50+ codes, 1998–2025)”
directory.intro: A comprehensive alphabetical listing of every Hyundai engine code from the last 25 years. Use the search above or enter your registration for an instant match to your specific engine. All codes link to a quote request.

engineYears.h2: Hyundai Engines by Year – What Was Fitted & When
engineYears.intro: This section covers Hyundai's complete UK engine timeline from 1998 to the present across every major model line - from the early Beta and Sirius diesel families through the Theta II controversy, the CRDi diesel era, the Smartstream generation, and the current hybrid and electric powertrain range - so owners can identify exactly what was fitted to their vehicle by year and compare rebuilt Hyundai engine prices from UK specialists.

years.preview: Beta Petrol & Early D4 Diesel Era
years.description: The late 1990s Hyundai UK range was built around the iron-block Beta four-cylinder petrol family alongside early D4 diesel units, establishing the foundations of the model range that would carry through to the Accent, Coupe, and first-generation Sonata.
keyChanges: G4GC Beta 2.0 16v four-cylinder dominant across Coupe, Sonata, and Elantra
G4EA Beta 1.6 introduced for entry-level Accent and Elantra applications
D4EA 2.0 CRDi diesel introduced (2000–2001) - Hyundai's first common-rail diesel for Sonata and early Santa Fe
Sirius D4BH 2.5 diesel continues in Terracan and H-1 commercial applications
Euro 2/3 in force across the UK range
mainEngines: G4GC - 2.0 petrol inline-four Beta (Coupe, Elantra, Sonata, Tucson early)
G4EA - 1.6 petrol inline-four Beta (Accent, Elantra)
D4EA - 2.0 CRDi diesel (Sonata, Santa Fe first gen, Elantra CRDi)
D4BH - 2.5 diesel (Terracan, H-1)
knownFor: G4GC Beta 2.0 is one of Hyundai's most durable petrol engines - iron block, conventional chain-driven DOHC; regularly achieves 150,000+ miles with basic maintenance; widely used in motorsport rebuilds

D4EA 2.0 CRDi marks Hyundai's credible entry into common-rail diesel - well-regarded for durability by UK used car specialists but injector O-ring seal leaks and EGR valve carbon deposits are the most commonly cited issues at high mileage
Beta family engines can suffer oil consumption from valve stem seal wear beyond 90,000 miles - a known trait across the G4GC and G4EA units
Early Hyundai UK models generate modest replacement engine demand - the real peak replacement volume arrives with the post-2006 CRDi fleet
years.cta: Find rebuilt 1998–2002 Hyundai Beta and CRDi engine prices from UK specialists

years.preview: Tucson & Santa Fe Diesel Volume Begins, G4KA/G4KC Theta Intro
years.description: The arrival of the first-generation Tucson (2004) and the second-generation Santa Fe brought Hyundai's 2.0 CRDi and 2.2 CRDi diesel engines to mass-market UK buyers, while the Theta four-cylinder petrol family was introduced to replace Beta in medium models.
keyChanges: Tucson (2004) launched with D4EA 2.0 CRDi diesel and G4GC petrol - Hyundai's first compact SUV in UK
D4EB 2.2 CRDi introduced (2005–2006) - higher-displacement diesel for Santa Fe and Sonata
Theta G4KC 2.4 and G4KA 2.0 introduced (2005) - aluminium block, new DOHC architecture replacing Beta in Sonata NF
Santa Fe second generation (2006) - 2.2 D4EB diesel and 3.3 Lambda V6 petrol options
Euro 4 enforcement begins
mainEngines: D4EA - 2.0 CRDi diesel (Tucson, Elantra, Sonata)
D4EB - 2.2 CRDi diesel (Santa Fe second gen, Sonata)
G4KC - 2.4 Theta petrol inline-four (Sonata NF)
G4KA - 2.0 Theta petrol inline-four (Sonata, Tucson petrol)
G4GC - 2.0 Beta petrol continued (Coupe, Elantra)
G6EA - 3.3 Lambda V6 petrol (Santa Fe)
knownFor: D4EA 2.0 CRDi in first-gen Tucson is the single highest-volume replacement engine search for Hyundai in the UK across this era - timing belt failure, EGR carbon fouling, and injector issues from 80,000 miles are the primary concerns for independent specialists
D4EB 2.2 CRDi generally regarded as more robust than D4EA - stronger architecture, but timing belt service intervals are critical and failures are catastrophic if neglected
Theta G4KC/G4KA petrol aluminium-block architecture is a welcome step forward in weight and refinement - however early units show oil consumption from piston ring wear at higher mileages
Lambda G6EA V6 petrol in Santa Fe considered smooth and durable but relatively rare in UK market - replacement demand limited
years.ticker: Common Replacement Enquiries: Hyundai Tucson 2.0 CRDi engine replacement - Hyundai D4EA engine replacement - Hyundai Santa Fe 2.2 diesel engine replacement - Hyundai Tucson engine timing belt failure
years.cta: Compare 2003–2006 Hyundai Tucson and Santa Fe diesel engine prices from UK specialists

years.preview: i30 & i40 Era, D4FB 1.6 CRDi Dominance & Gamma Petrol Introduction Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: The i30 (2007) transformed Hyundai's UK presence and brought the D4FB 1.6 CRDi into volume production as the most widely fitted Hyundai diesel in UK history - while the Gamma 1.4/1.6 petrol family introduced a modern all-aluminium entry-level architecture.
keyChanges: i30 (FD) launched (2007) with D4FB 1.6 CRDi diesel and G4FA/G4FC Gamma 1.4/1.6 petrol - volume sales begin 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction
Gamma G4FA 1.4 and G4FC 1.6 DOHC petrol introduced - aluminium block, chain-driven, replaces Alpha/Beta in small models
D4FB 1.6 CRDi becomes Hyundai's highest-volume diesel unit in UK market
i20 launched (2008) with Gamma petrol and D4FB diesel
Genesis Coupe launched (2009) with Theta G4KD 2.0T turbocharged petrol and Tau 3.8 V8
Euro 4 fully enforced; Euro 5 transition preparations begin
mainEngines: D4FB - 1.6 CRDi diesel (i30, i20, Elantra, Accent)
G4FA - 1.4 Gamma DOHC petrol (i20, i30, Accent)
G4FC - 1.6 Gamma DOHC petrol (i30, Elantra, Accent)
G4KD - 2.0 Theta II turbocharged petrol (Genesis Coupe, i40)
D4EA - 2.0 CRDi continued (Tucson, Santa Fe)
D4EB - 2.2 CRDi continued (Santa Fe)
knownFor: D4FB 1.6 CRDi is the definitive Hyundai UK diesel of the 2007–2015 era - fuel-efficient, torquey, and widely available; however timing chain wear between 80,000–120,000 km and EGR valve clogging are cited as the two most common UK specialist issues
Gamma G4FC/G4FA petrol units are reliable entry-level engines - oil consumption from valve stem seal wear beyond 80,000 miles is the primary long-term concern

D4FA/D4FB diesel EGR valve blockage in stop-start urban driving is an accelerated wear pattern widely discussed in UK Hyundai owner communities - regular motorway running recommended to extend EGR life

Genesis Coupe Theta G4KD turbocharged unit delivers genuinely sporty performance - rod bearing wear at high rpm under sustained hard use is an enthusiast-documented concern, foreshadowing the wider Theta II issues
years.ticker: Common Replacement Enquiries: Hyundai i30 1.6 CRDi engine replacement - Hyundai D4FB engine replacement - Hyundai i30 diesel engine timing chain - Hyundai i20 1.4 petrol engine replacement - Hyundai Santa Fe 2.2 diesel engine rebuild
years.cta: Get 2007–2010 Hyundai i30 and i20 engine replacement quotes from UK specialists

years.preview: Theta II Controversy, Nu Engine Launch & Euro 5 Transition Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: The Nu four-cylinder petrol family arrived as a modern replacement for Beta in medium models, but this period is defined globally by the Theta II crankshaft debris manufacturing defect - the largest and most consequential engine recall in Hyundai's history.
keyChanges: Theta II G4KH/G4KJ 2.0T and G4KE/G4KG 2.4 MPI engines under recall investigation - metal debris in crankshaft oil passages causes connecting rod bearing failure and engine seizure
Nu G4NA 2.0 and G4NB 1.8 introduced (2011) - replaces Beta in Elantra, i40; aluminium block, DOHC, Dual CVVT
i40 saloon and estate launched (2011) with Nu 2.0 petrol, 1.7 CRDi (D4FD) diesel
D4FD 1.7 CRDi introduced (2011) - new diesel for i40, ix35
ix35 launched (2010) with D4FB 1.6 CRDi and D4HA 2.0 CRDi - replaces Tucson
Euro 5 compliance in force from 2011
mainEngines: G4NA - 2.0 Nu DOHC petrol (Elantra MD, i40)
G4NB - 1.8 Nu DOHC petrol (Elantra, i40)
G4KH - 2.0 Theta II turbocharged petrol GDi (ix35 Turbo, Sonata)
D4FD - 1.7 CRDi diesel (i40, ix35)
D4HA - 2.0 CRDi diesel (ix35, Santa Fe, Sonata diesel)
D4FB - 1.6 CRDi (i30, i20, Accent continued)
knownFor: Theta II G4KH/G4KJ crankshaft debris defect is the most significant Hyundai engine engineering failure in the brand's history - machining swarf left in crankshaft oil drillings starves rod bearings of lubrication, leading to seizure and potential engine fire; recall covered 2011–2014 production

Nu G4NA 2.0 and G4NB 1.8 are significant improvements in refinement and economy over Beta - but early timing chain wear from 70,000 miles and oil consumption concerns are reported by UK and US owner forums
D4FD 1.7 CRDi in i40 delivers strong real-world economy but DPF regeneration issues in urban use and EGR valve clogging are the principal UK specialist complaints
D4HA 2.0 CRDi in ix35 is generally considered the most robust diesel in the range during this period - well regarded by specialist rebuilders for long-term durability
years.ticker: Common Replacement Enquiries: Hyundai Theta II engine replacement - Hyundai ix35 2.0 engine seizure - Hyundai i40 1.7 CRDi engine replacement - Hyundai 2.0 CRDi engine replacement ix35 - Hyundai Theta engine recall UK
years.cta: Get 2011–2013 Hyundai ix35, i40, and i30 replacement engine quotes from UK specialists

years.preview: Tucson NX1 & Santa Fe Third Gen, D4HA Euro 6 Prep & New i20 Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: The third-generation Tucson (NX1, 2015) and refreshed Santa Fe moved to updated Euro 5/6 CRDi units, the new Gamma T-GDi turbocharged petrol appeared in the i20 and i30, and continued Theta II recall fallout reshaped Hyundai's UK reputation.
keyChanges: Tucson third generation (NX1) launched (2015) with D4HA 2.0 CRDi and G4KH/G4NA petrol options - UK's most popular Hyundai SUV
Gamma G4FJ 1.6 T-GDi turbocharged petrol introduced (2015) - 177 hp; first hot hatch-spec Hyundai engine for i30 Turbo and i20 N precursor 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction
Kappa G3LC 1.0 T-GDi three-cylinder introduced (2015) - turbocharged entry-level petrol for i10 and i20
D4HA updated to Euro 6 specification (2015–2016) - revised EGR, DPF upgrade
Theta II recall expansion - additional production years of G4KH/G4KJ added to recall
New i20 (2014) with Gamma 1.4 G4FA and Kappa 1.2 G4LA petrol, plus D4FB diesel
mainEngines: D4HA - 2.0 CRDi diesel Euro 6 (Tucson NX1, Santa Fe, ix35 late)
G4FJ - 1.6 T-GDi turbocharged petrol (i30 Turbo, i20 performance variants)
G3LC - 1.0 T-GDi turbocharged three-cylinder petrol (i20, i10)
G4FA - 1.4 Gamma petrol (i20, i10)
G4NA - 2.0 Nu petrol (Tucson, Elantra)
D4FB - 1.6 CRDi continued (i30, i20)
knownFor: G4FJ 1.6 T-GDi is a genuinely exciting addition to the range - direct-injected, turbocharged, smooth power delivery; early units show carbon build-up on intake valves and some turbocharger wastegate rattle from 50,000–70,000 miles
D4HA Euro 6 Tucson is the best-received diesel Hyundai in UK independent specialist opinion - strong torque, reasonable long-term durability, but DPF and EGR maintenance essential for urban-use cars
Kappa G3LC 1.0 T-GDi three-cylinder is frugal and characterful but prone to excessive carbon build-up in stop-start use and oil dilution via DI system on short journeys

D4FB 1.6 CRDi continues as the highest-volume UK Hyundai diesel replacement engine - timing chain wear and EGR issues are unchanged from earlier specification; peak replacement demand for 2007–2011 cars hits through this period
years.ticker: Common Replacement Enquiries: Hyundai Tucson 2.0 diesel engine replacement 2015 - Hyundai D4HA engine replacement - Hyundai i30 1.6 T-GDi engine replacement - Hyundai i20 1.0 T-GDi engine problems - Hyundai ix35 2.0 CRDi engine replacement 2014
years.cta: Compare 2014–2016 Hyundai Tucson, Santa Fe, and i30 engine prices from UK specialists

years.preview: i30N Performance Launch, 1.6 MHEV Diesel & U3 1.6 CRDi Performance Landmark Technology Introduction
years.description: The i30 N hot hatch brought a purpose-built G4KH-derived performance engine to Hyundai for the first time, the Kona introduced the brand to compact crossover buyers with the full Gamma/Kappa petrol range, and Hyundai's first mild hybrid diesel appeared in the Tucson.
keyChanges: i30 N launched (2017) with G4KH 2.0 T-GDi 250/275 hp - first purpose-built Hyundai N performance engine 🔴 Performance Landmark
Kona launched (2017) - compact crossover with G3LC 1.0 T-GDi and G4FJ 1.6 T-GDi petrol, plus D4FB 1.6 CRDi diesel; Kona Electric BEV announced
1.6 CRDi MHEV (D4FE) introduced with 48V mild hybrid system in Tucson (2019) 🔴 Technology Introduction
Kona Electric (2018) - BEV with 39.2/64 kWh battery; Hyundai's first mass-market UK electric car
Euro 6d-TEMP enforcement - all new diesels require AdBlue/SCR from 2018–2019
Ioniq launched (2016–2017) - HEV, PHEV, and BEV in one model lineup; Hyundai's first dedicated electrified range
mainEngines: G4KH - 2.0 T-GDi turbocharged petrol (i30 N, Tucson, Veloster N)
G4FJ - 1.6 T-GDi petrol (Kona, i30, Tucson petrol)
G3LC - 1.0 T-GDi three-cylinder (Kona, i20, i10)
D4FE - 1.6 CRDi MHEV 48V diesel (Tucson, Kona diesel)
D4FB - 1.6 CRDi diesel (i30, i20, Kona diesel base)
Kona Electric motor - 136/204 hp BEV (Kona Electric)
Ioniq Hybrid - G4LE 1.6 GDi + electric motor HEV/PHEV
knownFor: i30 N G4KH 2.0 T-GDi is a landmark Hyundai performance engine - purpose-built with reinforced bottom end, dedicated cooling, and a mechanical limited-slip differential; UK enthusiast and specialist community considers it among the finest hot hatch engines in its class

1.6 CRDi MHEV D4FE delivers improved fuel economy and reduced urban emissions - however the 48V belt-starter-generator and MHEV control unit introduce new failure categories; dohc.co.uk reports significant MHEV variant diesel engine failures across both Hyundai and Kia applications
Ioniq HEV G4LE 1.6 GDi hybrid is notably efficient but uses an Atkinson-cycle combustion engine - low-mileage warm-up cycle oil dilution is a concern specific to hybrid applications
Kona Electric arrival signals the start of Hyundai's credible EV push in the UK - battery pack degradation and 12V battery failure are growing independent specialist areas as the first fleet ages
years.ticker: Common Replacement Enquiries: Hyundai i30 N engine replacement - Hyundai Tucson 1.6 MHEV diesel failure - Hyundai Kona 1.0 T-GDi engine problems - Hyundai D4FE engine replacement - Hyundai i30 N 2.0T engine rebuild UK
years.cta: Source 2017–2019 Hyundai i30 N, Kona, and Tucson replacement engine quotes today

years.preview: Smartstream Generation, Tucson NX4, IONIQ 5 & N Performance Peak Major Engine Family Introduction Technology Introduction
years.description: The Smartstream engine family replaced all previous Hyundai petrol and diesel architectures across the range from 2020, introducing CVVD continuous variable valve duration technology, while the IONIQ 5 launched as the brand's first dedicated BEV platform.
keyChanges: Smartstream G4NL 2.0 MPi, G4NJ 2.0 MPi, and G1.6 T-GDi replace Nu and Gamma families from 2020 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction
Tucson fourth generation (NX4) launched (2020–2021) - Smartstream petrol and diesel, PHEV (230e) and HEV options
IONIQ 5 (2021) - dedicated E-GMP electric platform, 58/72.6 kWh battery; 800V ultra-fast charging 🔴 Technology Introduction
Smartstream Diesel 1.6 CRDi replaces D4FE in Tucson NX4 and Kona - Euro 6d final compliant
Hyundai i20 N launched (2021) - G4CG 1.6 T-GDi 204 hp; second N performance model for UK
CVVD (Continuously Variable Valve Duration) technology introduced in Smartstream G4FG 1.6 T-GDi - world-first production application
mainEngines: Smartstream G4NL - 2.0 MPi petrol (Tucson NX4, Elantra CN7)
Smartstream G4FG / G4FJ update - 1.6 T-GDi with CVVD (Tucson PHEV/HEV, i30, i20 N)
Smartstream D 1.6 CRDi (D4FE update) - 1.6 diesel (Tucson NX4, Kona diesel)
Smartstream D 2.0 CRDi (D4HA update) - 2.0 diesel (Santa Fe)
G4KH - 2.0 T-GDi continued (i30 N, Veloster N)
IONIQ 5 Electric Motor - 170/225 hp BEV, E-GMP (IONIQ 5)
Tucson PHEV - G4FG 1.6 T-GDi + 13.8 kWh battery + 91 hp electric motor
knownFor: Smartstream engines represent Hyundai's most advanced combustion architecture to date - CVVD technology in G4FG is genuinely innovative and delivers measurable fuel economy gains versus predecessor Gamma units

Tucson NX4 PHEV delivers strong performance and economy in combined use - but high-voltage battery degradation and pure-EV range reduction are emerging UK concerns as these vehicles pass 40,000–50,000 miles

IONIQ 5 launch is a transformative moment for Hyundai - 800V charging and class-leading range; battery degradation has been slower than expected, reinforcing Hyundai's EV reputation with UK long-term owners and fleet operators

1.6 CRDi MHEV diesel failures continue to be reported on 2019–2021 production vehicles - oil contamination and seized engines reported on low-mileage examples across Tucson and Kona; growing UK Facebook and specialist forum concern
years.ticker: Common Replacement Enquiries: Hyundai Tucson 1.6 diesel engine replacement 2021 - Hyundai MHEV diesel engine failure - Hyundai i30 N engine replacement 2020 - Hyundai Tucson PHEV engine problems - Hyundai Santa Fe diesel engine replacement 2020
years.cta: Get 2020–2022 Hyundai Tucson, IONIQ 5, and i30 N engine replacement quotes from UK specialists

years.preview: IONIQ 6, New Kona Generation, N Brand Maturity & Final Combustion Iterations
years.description: Hyundai's UK range continues to expand its electrified and BEV offerings under the IONIQ sub-brand, while combustion engine models carry on with Smartstream petrol and diesel in the Tucson, Santa Fe, and i30 lines - and N performance models reach maturity.
keyChanges: IONIQ 6 launched (2023) - E-GMP platform, 77.4 kWh battery, up to 614km WLTP range; class-leading aerodynamics
New Kona second generation (2023) - full BEV, PHEV, and 1.0 T-GDi/1.6 T-GDi petrol options on new platform
Santa Fe fifth generation (2023) - new platform, PHEV 1.6 T-GDi and HEV variants; no diesel option in UK from 2024
Hyundai phases diesel engines out of passenger car range (2024 strategy) - Smartstream diesel final applications
i30 N and Kona N continue with updated G4KH 2.0 T-GDi
IONIQ 7 (2024–2025) announced for UK - three-row EV SUV on E-GMP platform
mainEngines: Smartstream G4FG 1.6 T-GDi PHEV/HEV - (Kona PHEV, Tucson HEV, Santa Fe PHEV)
Smartstream G3LC / G4NL - 1.0 T-GDi / 2.0 petrol (Kona petrol, i30 base)
G4KH - 2.0 T-GDi (i30 N, Kona N - continuing)
IONIQ 6 Electric motor - 168/239 hp (RWD/AWD BEV)
IONIQ 5 N motor - 650 hp dual-motor performance BEV 🔴 Performance Landmark
Smartstream D 1.6 CRDi - final applications (Tucson - phasing out)
knownFor: IONIQ 5 N is a transformative performance landmark - 650 hp dual-motor BEV with N Grin Shift overboost and simulated gearshift via N e-shift; UK performance community considers it among the most exciting driver's cars launched in 2024 regardless of powertrain

Santa Fe diesel exit from 2024 marks a significant strategic shift - owners of existing diesel Santa Fe and Tucson models now supported by a strong UK independent specialist aftermarket as factory support migrates to electrified variants
1.6 CRDi MHEV engines on 2019–2022 vehicles represent the primary near-term UK replacement engine demand category - failure rates on this specific unit remain a concern at UK specialist level

Smartstream PHEV and HEV systems are maturing well - long-term durability data is emerging positively, but high-voltage battery replacement costs (£3,000–£6,000 at UK specialists) are a growing ownership concern for first-generation Tucson PHEV and Ioniq PHEV owners
years.cta: Compare current Hyundai Smartstream, N-series, and IONIQ electric engine prices from UK specialists

engineYears.closing: Not sure which year your Hyundai was built? Enter your registration number and we'll identify the exact engine, model year, and compatible replacement options instantly - all backed by a minimum 12-month warranty from UK Hyundai specialists.

faq.h2: Hyundai Engine Replacement – Frequently Asked Questions
faq.intro: Quick, honest answers to the most common questions Hyundai owners ask before comparing engine replacement prices and finding a UK specialist.

items.question: How much does a Hyundai engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A used Hyundai engine typically costs £400–£900, while a fully reconditioned unit ranges from £1,200–£2,800 depending on your exact engine code and model. Supply-and-fit labour adds £600–£1,200, which remains significantly cheaper than main dealer Hyundai engine replacement cost estimates that often exceed £3,500. We always recommend requesting three tailored quotes to secure the best cost of Hyundai engine uk rate for your i30, Tucson, or Santa Fe.
items.cta: Compare Hyundai engine replacement prices now

items.question: What affects the cost of a Hyundai engine replacement?
items.answer: Four main factors dictate your final bill: engine displacement, unit condition, supply-only versus full installation, and your workshop’s location (London and South East rates run 20–30% higher). Older naturally aspirated models like the first-generation ix35 often carry a lower Hyundai engine fitting cost uk than newer turbocharged or hybrid variants. Our free comparison tool instantly filters transparent Hyundai engine supply and fit cost options so you avoid hidden mark-ups.
items.cta: Use our price comparison tool to find your nearest Hyundai engine specialists

items.question: Is it worth replacing the engine in my Hyundai?
items.answer: In most cases, yes — especially if your chassis and gearbox remain in solid condition. A rebuilt unit fitted with upgraded bearings or revised timing components typically outlasts the factory original, making it far more economical than purchasing a newer car. If you're weighing an engine rebuild vs replacement uk decision for a well-maintained i10 or i40, replacement preserves your vehicle’s value and extends its life by another 100k+ miles.
items.cta: Use our free comparison tool to see whether a rebuilt or used Hyundai engine makes financial sense

items.question: What's included in a Hyundai engine supply and fit service?
items.answer: A complete Hyundai engine supply and fit service covers sourcing a verified replacement, removing the faulty block, installing the new unit, and refilling all fluids, filters, and coolants. Reputable specialists also include a workmanship warranty, safe disposal of your old engine, and a comprehensive road test before handover. Labour for this bundled service typically runs £600–£1,200, so always confirm exactly what your Hyundai engine installation cost quote includes.
items.cta: Compare Hyundai engine supply and fit deals from vetted UK specialists

items.question: How long does a Hyundai engine replacement take?
items.answer: A straightforward swap on popular models like the i30 or Tucson generally takes 3–5 working days. More complex variants, such as CRDi diesels or early TMED hybrids, can require 5–7 days due to additional ancillary fitting and ECU coding. Engine availability is the biggest variable, but our UK network actively stocks high-demand codes like the D4FB and G4NA to minimise wait times.
items.cta: Ask suppliers about current lead times before booking your Hyundai engine swap

items.question: Do reconditioned Hyundai engines come with warranty?
items.answer: Yes, every reconditioned Hyundai engine supplied through our marketplace includes a minimum 12-month unlimited mileage warranty as standard. Many independent rebuilders also offer extended 24-month coverage on premium remanufactured units for added peace of mind. This warranty typically covers major internal components like the crankshaft, pistons, timing assembly, and oil pump, though you should always confirm exact Hyundai engine warranty uk terms directly with your chosen supplier.
items.cta: Find reconditioned Hyundai engines with comprehensive warranty cover today

items.question: Should I choose a used or reconditioned Hyundai engine?
items.answer: A used Hyundai engine offers the lowest upfront cost, making it a sensible choice if you plan to sell soon or need an immediate budget fix. However, a reconditioned unit costs slightly more while delivering new seals, gaskets, upgraded internals, and a full warranty for another 100,000+ miles. Given known issues like Theta II bearing wear, a reconditioned Hyundai engine is strongly recommended for long-term owners who prioritise reliability.
items.cta: Compare used vs recon Hyundai engine prices to find your best option

items.question: Where can I find Hyundai engine specialists near me?
items.answer: EngineMarket instantly connects you with a verified UK-wide network, ensuring you’re never far from qualified Hyundai engine specialists uk. While independent rebuilders are particularly concentrated across the Midlands and Northern regions, our registration lookup tool matches your exact engine code to nearby suppliers with immediate stock. Nationwide courier delivery is also available if a closer hub lacks your specific Gamma or CRDi unit.
items.cta: Get quotes from UK Hyundai engine specialists covering your local area

items.question: What are the most common Hyundai engine failures?
items.answer: The most frequent catastrophic issues include connecting rod bearing failure on 2.0/2.4L Theta II petrol engines, timing chain stretch in 1.4–1.6L Gamma units, and severe oil dilution from blocked DPF/EGR systems on 1.6L CRDi diesels. Turbocharger oil-coking also affects early Kappa T-GDi engines driven primarily on short urban trips. Because these Hyundai engine failure symptoms rarely allow for safe preventative repair, full replacement is usually the most reliable long-term solution.
items.cta: Compare rebuilt Hyundai engine prices before minor damage becomes catastrophic

items.question: How can I get the best price on a Hyundai engine?
items.answer: Prices for identical Hyundai engine codes can vary by over £1,000 between independent suppliers, so comparing at least three quotes is essential for securing the cheapest way to replace engine uk. Opt for a low-mileage used Hyundai engine if you need an immediate budget solution, or choose a reconditioned unit with upgraded internals for long-term reliability. Always verify whether your quote includes VAT, delivery, and ancillary fittings before committing to lock in the best price Hyundai engine deal available.
items.cta: Compare Hyundai engine prices now and secure your best deal today
